title: Handling Mask Imbalance
concept: Solving mask imbalance requires combining photometric augmentations, patch-based sampling, and overlap-based loss functions.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does the thresholding step do at the end of a segmentation pipeline?

options.text: It converts pixel probability scores into a binary 0 or 1 mask
It reduces the number of channels in the convolutional layers
It calculates the average IoU score across classes

exercises.explain: Correct! Thresholding maps probabilities (e.g., >0.5) to binary masks.

cards.title: Imbalance in the wild
cards.body: You are segmenting solar panels on roofs using satellite images. 98% of the pixels in your dataset are grass, trees, and roads. Only 2% are solar panels. Your model keeps outputting empty masks, ignoring the panels. We need to implement our imbalance strategies. How do we tune the pipeline to fix this?

cards.title: Imbalance Mitigation
cards.body:
To handle Mask Imbalance in production:
1. **Data level**: Use target-biased patch sampling.
2. **Augmentation level**: Apply elastic transforms and random crops around targets.
3. **Loss level**: Use Focal + Dice hybrid loss.

cards.title: A specialized training camp
cards.body: If a soccer player is bad at penalty kicks, you don't make them run laps all day (random background data). You set up a drill where they practice only penalty kicks over and over (biased sampling) until they master it.

cards.title: Solar panel pipeline adjustments
cards.body: We filter our satellite images. During training, we ensure that every batch contains at least 50% images with solar panels, and we crop them directly around the roofs. We train using Dice loss, ignoring the background size.

cards.title: Augmenting masks is different
cards.body: You might think you can augment the input image and leave the mask alone. That's a huge error! If you rotate or crop the image, you must apply the **exact same geometric transformation** to the ground truth mask, or the labels will be misaligned.

cards.title: Imbalance Solutions
cards.body: How to fight class imbalance at multiple levels:

left.text:
Filter dataset for target classes
Crop and zoom around target boundaries
Apply identical transforms to image and mask

right.text:
Switch to Focal Loss (suppress background)
Use Dice Loss (directly optimize overlap)
Combine as Hybrid Loss

exercises.prompt: If you rotate a training image by 45 degrees, what must you do to the ground truth mask?

options.text: Rotate it by the exact same 45 degrees
Convert it to a bounding box

exercises.explain: Correct! Geometric transforms must be applied identically to both image and mask to maintain alignment.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using Dice loss is a data-level strategy for handling mask imbalance.
exercises.explain: False. Dice loss is a loss-level strategy; biased sampling is a data-level strategy.

exercises.sentence: Target-biased patch sampling forces the model to train on crops containing the ___ class.
exercises.explain: It ensures the model frequently observes the rare target class features.

exercises.prompt: Which loss combination is highly recommended for training on extremely small target masks?
exercises.explain: Focal + Dice is the industry standard hybrid loss for imbalanced masks.

exercises.prompt: You are training a model to segment wildfire smoke plumes in satellite photos. The smoke is rare. The model is predicting no smoke at all. Which combination of changes should you apply?

options.text: Implement target-biased patch sampling to feed smoke-filled crops, and switch the loss function to Dice + Focal loss.
Use random crops, standard cross-entropy loss, and increase the image resolution.
Flatten the images and use a simple classification network with mean squared error.
Apply rotation to the image only, keeping the mask completely unrotated.

exercises.explain: Perfect! Combining target-biased sampling with a robust hybrid loss is the standard recipe for forcing models to learn rare, sparse classes like smoke. Next: compressing models for real-time edge use!
